skill.name: slack-gif-creator
skill.description: Create and optimize animated GIFs for Slack messages and custom emoji using Python image utilities, animation templates, and size validators.
skill.summary: Build Slack-ready animated GIFs with templates, effects, and validation helpers.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are false positives from Markdown code fences, animation math terms, Apache license text, and documentation wording. No evidence found of network clients, command execution, credential access, dynamic eval, obfuscation, or prompt injection. The confirmed risk is limited local filesystem access for saving generated GIFs and validating existing GIF files.

low_findings.title: Local GIF File Read and Write Operations
low_findings.description: The skill writes generated GIFs to a caller-provided output path and reads local GIF files for validation. This is expected for a GIF creation tool, but users should choose output paths carefully and avoid validating untrusted files in sensitive directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly uses Path, stat, Image.open, and imageio.imwrite on local GIF paths. The behavior is semantically necessary for the tool and no exfiltration or command execution path was found.

low_findings.title: Template Import Path Modification
low_findings.description: Several animation templates append the repository parent directory to sys.path so they can import local core modules. This is a common local-template pattern, but it can load unintended modules if run from a manipulated checkout.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sys.path pattern is visible in template modules and is used for local imports. I did not find evidence that it downloads code, executes commands, or intentionally loads attacker-controlled modules.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Weak-Crypto and C2 Matches Were False Positives
dangerous_patterns.description: The reported weak cryptographic and C2 indicators occur in prose, animation easing math, Apache license terms, and GIF optimization messages. No cryptographic API, malware command channel, encoded payload, or remote endpoint behavior was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are non-security contexts: easing constants, user-facing optimization text, license boilerplate, and documentation examples. No code path uses MD5, SHA1, beaconing, callback infrastructure, or command-and-control logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Code Fences Were Misread as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The repeated external-command detections in SKILL.md are Markdown fenced examples for Python and Bash usage. They are documentation snippets, not Ruby backtick execution or runtime command dispatch inside the skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines are inside Markdown code fences and are intended instructions or examples. The only shell command is a dependency installation instruction, not hidden execution by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Apache License URLs Were Misread as Network Behavior
dangerous_patterns.description: The hardcoded URLs detected in LICENSE.txt are standard Apache License reference links. No code imports network libraries or sends data to these URLs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in license text and there is no corresponding HTTP client or network call. This is a clear documentation-only false positive.

content.value_statement: Slack GIFs and custom emoji often fail because they exceed size, frame, or dimension limits. This skill provides templates, effects, and validators to create compact animated GIFs for Slack.
actual_capabilities: Creates animated GIF frames from emojis, text, shapes, and simple visual objects.
Provides reusable motion templates such as bounce, shake, spin, slide, fade, pulse, zoom, and morph.
Optimizes GIF color counts, dimensions, frame counts, and duplicate frames for smaller Slack uploads.
Validates local GIF size and dimensions against Slack message and custom emoji constraints.
Adds visual effects such as outlines, shadows, particles, impact flashes, motion blur, and glow.
Includes palette and typography helpers for more consistent animated GIF design.
limitations: It does not upload GIFs to Slack or use the Slack API.
It requires Python image dependencies such as Pillow, imageio, and numpy.
Emoji rendering quality depends on fonts installed on the local system.
Complex animations may still need manual simplification to meet Slack custom emoji size limits.

use_cases.title: Custom Reaction Emoji
use_cases.description: Create small animated reaction emoji that stay near Slack custom emoji size limits.

use_cases.title: Team Announcement GIF
use_cases.description: Build a short message GIF with text, motion, and effects for a Slack announcement.

use_cases.title: Prototype Animation Concepts
use_cases.description: Generate quick animation drafts using reusable movement and effect templates.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Emoji GIF
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a compact custom emoji.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Slack custom emoji GIF of a smiling face bouncing gently. Keep it 128 by 128 pixels and validate the final size.

prompt_templates.title: Message Reaction Animation
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for a larger message GIF.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Slack message GIF with a rocket launching, a short burst of particles, and the text "Shipped". Optimize it for a Slack message upload.

prompt_templates.title: Branded Team Moment
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a polished Slack GIF for a product launch using blue and green colors, a pulse effect, and readable outlined text. Validate dimensions and file size.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Phase Animation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request with several animation phases.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a three-phase Slack GIF: anticipation, impact, and celebration. Combine move, shake, flash, and confetti effects, then reduce colors and frames until it is Slack ready.

output_examples.input: A tiny animated celebration emoji for Slack.
output_examples.output: A 128 by 128 pixel celebration GIF with confetti, motion, reduced colors, and a validation result showing whether it fits the custom emoji limit.

output_examples.input: A larger launch announcement GIF for a Slack channel.
output_examples.output: A square message GIF with a rocket motion path, impact flash, outlined launch text, and size guidance for Slack upload.
A short shaking emoji GIF with limited frames, simple background color, and optimization suggestions if the file is too large.

best_practices: Start with simple motion and low frame counts when targeting Slack custom emoji.
Validate file size after each major design change instead of only at the end.
Use outlined text and limited palettes to preserve readability after GIF compression.
anti_patterns: Do not use long durations or high frame rates for Slack custom emoji.
Do not rely on gradients or detailed backgrounds when the file must stay under 64 KB.
Do not assume emoji fonts render the same on every operating system.

faq.question: Does this skill upload GIFs to Slack?
faq.answer: No. It creates and validates local GIF files, but upload is handled outside the skill.

faq.question: Can it create Slack custom emoji?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes 128 by 128 pixel guidance and size checks for Slack custom emoji GIFs.

faq.question: What dependencies does it need?
faq.answer: It uses Python image libraries, including Pillow, imageio, and numpy.

faq.question: Can it make text animations?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes text rendering helpers with outlines, shadows, and reusable motion templates.

faq.question: Why might an emoji GIF still be too large?
faq.answer: Slack custom emoji have a strict size limit. Reduce frames, colors, effects, and background detail.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with low risk. Review found no malicious behavior, network calls, command execution, or prompt injection.
